answer:
**"The Wife's Lament"** (ch. 3 of *Old English Elegies*) is the speaker's monologue of profound isolation and grief following a forced separation from her lord, plotted by his kinsmen amid a feud; she dwells alone "under an oak tree in this earth-cave," weeping endlessly for her exile.[^1] Its most distinctive line opens the poem: "I shape this lay of my own deep sorrow, my own life’s journey."[^2]

**"The Husband's Message"** (ch. 8) is delivered by a carved wooden tablet acting as messenger from the now-prosperous lord (husband), exiled by the same "feud" that sundered the lovers; it recalls their past vows ("that you two in old days often spoke"), promises "steadfast troth," wealth, and reunion, and seals the oath with runes ᛋᚱᛠᚹᛞ ("Sun-Ride, Sea-Joy, Day").[^3] Its most distinctive line is the runic oath: "I join together ᛋ and ᚱ, and ᛠ, ᚹ, and ᛞ to name in an oath [Sun-Ride, Sea-Joy, Day]." (Note: the translation shows lacunae from the damaged manuscript, but the runes are clear.)

These voices complement each other thematically—both rooted in *wraec* (exile) from feud, lost homeland, and longing vows—but they are not in direct dialogue. The wife's lament fixates on irreparable misery and solitary suffering ("I can never find any rest from my heart’s-care"), with no hint of hope or outreach.[^4] The husband's message, years later, directly addresses a "treasure-adorned" woman (her), recalling their shared past and urging her sea-voyage south for joy ("No greater joy in this world could come to him... than if... you two... together"). They evoke the same events from opposite perspectives (her static despair vs. his active renewal), but speak *past* each other: she sees no path forward, while he offers one she never voices.

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3, [full chapter](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3, [p-23cb49](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/#p-23cb49).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 8, [p-58bec2](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/08-nu-ic-onsundran-the-secgan-wille-treocyn-ic-tudre-/translation/#p-58bec2).
[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3, [p-c1cc32](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/#p-c1cc32).
